elements.sent-2411-129: We present a 4D street gaussian splatting representation for self-supervised static-dynamic decomposition and high-fidelity surface reconstruction without the requirement for extra 3D annotations such as bounding boxes.
elements.sent-2411-130: We present \ourmethod, a self-supervised gaussian splatting representation, enabling effective static-dynamic decomposition and high-fidelity surface reconstruction in complex driving scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-131: Our approach employs a two-stage optimization pipeline of dynamic street Gaussians.
elements.sent-2411-132: In the first stage, we extract 2D motion masks based on the observation that 3D Gaussian Splatting inherently can reconstruct only the static regions in dynamic environments.
elements.sent-2411-133: These extracted 2D motion priors are then mapped into the Gaussian space in a differentiable manner, leveraging an efficient formulation of dynamic Gaussians in the second stage.
elements.sent-2411-134: Combined with the introduced geometric regularizations, our method are able to address the over-fitting issues caused by data sparsity in autonomous driving, reconstructing physically plausible Gaussians that align with object surfaces rather than floating in air.
elements.sent-2411-135: Furthermore, we introduce temporal cross-view consistency to ensure coherence across time and viewpoints, resulting in high-quality surface reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-136: Comprehensive experiments demonstrate the efficiency and effectiveness of \ourmethod, surpassing prior self-supervised arts and achieving accuracy comparable to methods relying on external 3D bounding box annotations.
elements.sent-2411-138: Modeling driving scenes \cite{kitti_2012_cvpr, waymo_2020_cvpr is essential for autonomous driving applications, as it facilitates real-world simulation and supports scene understanding \cite{hugs_2024_cvpr .}}
elements.sent-2411-139: An effective scene representation enables a system to efficiently perceive and reconstruct dynamic driving environments.
elements.sent-2411-140: Recent 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) \cite{3dgs_2023_TOG has emerged as a prominent 3D representation that can be optimized through 2D supervision.}
elements.sent-2411-141: It has gained popularity due to its explicit nature, high efficiency, and rendering speed.
elements.sent-2411-142: While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has demonstrated strong performance in static object-centric reconstructions, the original 3DGS struggles to handle dynamic objects in unbounded street views, which are common in real-world scenarios, particularly for autonomous driving applications.
elements.sent-2411-143: It is unable to effectively model dynamic regions, leading to blurring artifacts due to the Gaussian model's time-independent parameterization.
elements.sent-2411-144: As a result, 4D-GS~\cite{4dgs_2024_cvpr is proposed, modeling the dynamics with a Hexplane encoder.}
elements.sent-2411-145: The Hexplane \cite{hexplane_2023_cvpr works well on object-level datasets, but struggles with driving scenes because of the unbounded areas in outdoor environments.}
elements.sent-2411-146: Instead, we choose to reformulate the original static Gaussian model as time-dependent variables with minor changes, ensuring the efficiency of handling large-scale driving scenes.
elements.sent-2411-147: In this paper, we present \ourmethod, a purely Gaussian Splatting-based representation, which facilitates self-supervised static-dynamic decomposition and high-quality surface reconstruction in driving scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-148: For static-dynamic decomposition, existing methods such as DrivingGaussian \cite{drivinggaussian_2024_cvpr and Street Gaussians \cite{streetgs_2024_eccv , rely on explicit 3D bounding boxes, which significantly simplifies the decomposition problem, since dynamic Gaussians in a moving bounding box can be simply removed.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Without the 3D annotations, some recent self-supervised methods like PVG \cite{pvg_2023_arxiv and S3Gaussian \cite{s3g_2024_arxiv have attempted to achieve decomposition but fall short in performance, as they treat all Gaussians as dynamic, relying on indirect supervision to learn motion patterns.}}
elements.sent-2411-150: However, our proposed method can achieve effective self-supervised decomposition, based on a simple observation that dynamic regions reconstructed from 3DGS are blurry---quite different from the ground truth images.
elements.sent-2411-151: Despite the absence of 3D annotations, \ourmethod produces results comparable to, or better than, approaches that use explicit bounding boxes for decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-152: Another challenge in applying 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) to autonomous driving is the sparse nature of images, which is more pronounced compared to object-centric reconstruction tasks.
elements.sent-2411-153: This sparsity often leads 3DGS to overfit on the limited number of observations, resulting in inaccurate geometry learning.
elements.sent-2411-154: Inspired by 2D Gaussian Splatting (2DGS) \cite{2dgs_2024_siggraph , we aim to generate flatter, disk-shaped Gaussians to better align with the surfaces of objects like roads and walls.}
elements.sent-2411-155: We also couple the normal and scale of each Gaussian, which can be optimized jointly to improve surface reconstruction quality.
elements.sent-2411-156: To further address the overfitting issue, we propose temporal geometrical cross-view consistency, which significantly enhances the model’s geometric awareness and accuracy by aggregating information from different views across time.
elements.sent-2411-157: These strategies allow us to achieve state-of-the-art reconstruction quality, surpassing other Gaussian splatting approaches in the field of autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-159: We propose to extract motion information easily from appearance differences based on a simple observation that 3DGS cannot successfully model the dynamic regions.
elements.sent-2411-160: We then distill the extracted 2D motion priors in local frames into global gaussian space, using time-varying Gaussians in a differentiable manner.
elements.sent-2411-161: We introduce effective 3D regularizations and temporal cross-view consistency to generate physically reasonable Gaussian ellipsoids, further enhancing high-quality decomposition and reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-162: We demonstrate \ourmethod's capability of effective static-dynamic decomposition and high-fidelity surface reconstruction across various challenging datasets \cite{waymo_2020_cvpr, kitti_2012_cvpr .}

elements.sent-2411-198: Periodic Vibration Gaussian (PVG): PVG \cite{pvg_2023_arxiv reshapes the original Gaussian model by introducing time-dependent adjustments to the position mean \(\boldsymbol{\mu}\) and opacity \(o\).}
elements.sent-2411-199: The modified model is represented as follows: \begin{equation}\tilde{\boldsymbol{\mu}}(t) = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \frac{l}{2\pi} \cdot \sin\left( \frac{2\pi (t - \tau)}{l} \right) \cdot \boldsymbol{v},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\tilde{o}(t) = o \cdot e^{-\frac{1}{2} (t - \tau)^2 \beta^{-2}},\end{equation} where \(\tilde{\boldsymbol{\mu (t)\) denotes vibrating position centered at \(\boldsymbol{\mu}\) occurring around life peak \(\tau\), and \(\tilde{o (t)\) represents the time-dependent opacity which undergoes exponential decay as time deviates from the life peak \(\tau\).}}}
elements.sent-2411-200: Thus, the PVG model is expressed as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{G}(t) = \{\tilde{\boldsymbol{\mu}}(t), \boldsymbol{s}, \boldsymbol{r}, \tilde{o}(t), \boldsymbol{c}, \tau, \beta, \boldsymbol{v}\},\end{equation} We adopt PVG as the dynamic representation for autonomous driving scenes, because PVG model preserves the structure of the original 3D GS model at any given time $t$, enabling it to be rendered using the standard 3D GS pipeline to reconstruct the dynamic scene.

elements.sent-2411-205: During the first stage, we observe that 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) performs effectively in reconstructing static elements, such as parked cars and buildings in a driving scene.
elements.sent-2411-206: However, it struggles to accurately reconstruct dynamic regions, as the original 3DGS does not incorporate temporal information.

elements.sent-2411-208: To address this issue, we leverage the significant differences between static and dynamic regions to develop an efficient method for extracting segmentation masks that encode motion information.
elements.sent-2411-209: Initially, a pretrained foundation model is employed to extract features from both the rendered image and the ground truth (GT) image used for supervision.
elements.sent-2411-210: Let $\hat{F}$ denote the features extracted from the rendered image $\hat{I}$, and $F$ represent the features extracted from the GT image $I$.
elements.sent-2411-211: To distinguish dynamic and static regions, we compute the per-pixel dissimilarity $D$ between the corresponding features.
elements.sent-2411-212: The dissimilarity metric $D$ approaches 0 for similar features, indicating static regions, and nears 1 for dissimilar features, corresponding to dynamic regions.. \begin{equation}D = \left( 1 - cos( \hat{F}, F) \right) / 2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-213: As the pretrained model is frozen, the resulting dissimilarity score $D \in R^{H \times W}$ is computed without involving any learnable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-214: Rather than applying a simple threshold to $D$ to generate a motion segmentation mask, we propose a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) decoder to predict the dynamicness $\delta \in R^{H \times W}$.
elements.sent-2411-215: This decoder leverages the extracted features, which contain rich semantic information, while the dissimilarity score is employed to guide and optimize the learning process of the decoder. \begin{equation}\label{eq:L-d} \mathcal{L}_{dyn} = \delta \odot D,\end{equation} where $\odot$ refers to the element-wise multiplication.
elements.sent-2411-216: By employing the loss function $\mathcal{L _{dyn}$ defined in Eq. \ref{eq:L-d , the decoder is optimized to predict lower values in regions where $D$ is high, corresponding to dynamic regions, thereby minimizing the loss.}}

elements.sent-2411-307: In this paper, we propose \ourmethod, a self-supervised approach for static-dynamic decomposition and high-quality surface reconstruction in driving scenes.

elements.sent-2411-342: StreetSurf~\cite{streetsurf_2023_arxiv is an implicit neural rendering method for both geometry and appearance reconstruction in street views.}

elements.sent-2411-346: It contains a background node approximating all static parts, several dynamic nodes representing rigidly moving individuals, and edges representing transformations.
elements.sent-2411-347: NSG\cite{nsg_2021_cvpr also combines implicitly encoded scenes with a jointly learned latent representation to describe objects in a single implicit function.}
elements.sent-2411-348: SUDS~\cite{suds_2023_cvpr is a NeRF-based method for dynamic large urban scene reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-349: It proposed using 2D optical flow to model scene dynamics, avoiding additional bounding box annotations.
elements.sent-2411-350: SUDS develops a three-branch hash table representation for 4D scene representation, enabling a variety of downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-351: StreetGS~\cite{streetgs_2024_eccv models dynamic driving scenes using 3D Gaussian splatting.}
elements.sent-2411-352: It represents the components in the scene separately, with a background model for static part and an object model for foreground moving objects.
elements.sent-2411-353: To capture dynamic features, the position and rotation of gaussians are defined in an object local coordinate system, which relies on bounding boxes predicted by an off-the-shelf model.
elements.sent-2411-354: HUGS~\cite{hugs_2024_cvpr is a 3DGS-based method addressing the problem of urban scene reconstruction and understanding.}
elements.sent-2411-355: It assumes that the scene is composed of static rigions and moving vehicles with rigid motions, using a unicycle model to model vehicles' states.
elements.sent-2411-356: HUGS also extends original 3DGS to model additional modalities, including optical flow and semantic information, achieving good performance in both scene reconstruction and semantic reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-357: Bounding boxes are also required in this process.
elements.sent-2411-358: EmerNeRF~\cite{emernerf_2023_arxiv is a NeRF-based method for constructing 4D neural scene representations in urban driving scenes.}
elements.sent-2411-359: It decomposes dynamic scenes into a static field and a dynamic field, both parameterized by hash grids.
elements.sent-2411-360: Then an emergent scene flow field is introduced to represent explicit correspondences between moving objects and aggregate temporally-displaced features.
elements.sent-2411-361: Remarkably, EmerNeRF finishes these tasks all through self-supervision.
elements.sent-2411-362: S3Gaussian~\cite{s3g_2024_arxiv is a self-supervised approach that decomposes static and dynamic 3D gaussians in driving scenes.}
elements.sent-2411-363: It aggregates 4D gaussian representations in a spatial-temporal field network with a multi-resolution hexplane encoder, where the dynamic objects are visible only within spatial-temporal plane while static objects within spatial-only plane.
elements.sent-2411-364: Then S3Gaussian utilizes a multi-head decoder to capture the deformation of 3D Gaussians in a canonical space for decomposition.
